Drafted by Kanter. Copied to Quayle and Sununu. A stamped notation indicates Bush saw the memorandum on May 14.

(p) d. Subject to a mutually acceptable definition of "as equipped," the U.S. may agree that any U.S. heavy bombers equipped for nuclear-armed ALCMs in excess of 150-180 will count against the 6000 warhead limit on an “as equipped" basis. . ye as. - at bere Cas? BY Beams BD 20587
THE WHITE HOUSE '
WASHINGTON
° ) May 14, 1990; mS
: PER E.0. 13526
LP/LM aoy-ooy
NATIONAL SECURITY DIRECTIVE 40;;
-- Fl 4laly
MEMORANDUM FOR THE VICE PRESIDENT
. THE SECRETARY OF STATE:
, ' THE SECRETARY OF DEFENSE:
. THE SECRETARY OF ENERGY
THE DIRECTOR, OFFICE OF MANAGEMENT AND BUDGET
. THE ASSISTANT TO THE PRESIDENT FOR NATIONAL
, SECURITY AFFAIRS
. THE DIRECTOR OF CENTRAL INTELLIGENCE:
THE CHAIRMAN, JOINT CHIEFS OF STAFF
. THE DIRECTOR, ARMS CONTROL AND DISARMAMENT AGENCY
SUBJECT: Decisions on START Issues (U)
At the upcoming U.S.-Soviet summit, I hope. to reach substantive.
. agreement on all of the major START issues on a basis that will
advance U.S. national security interests and promote'
international stability. The following decisions are designed to
help achieve that goal by making a best faith effort to resolve
the issues that they address. (Z)
1. ALCMs and SLCMs. Building on current areas of agreement
with the Soviet Union and existing guidance, the U.S. can accept ~
'a package that includes the following elements:.
ALCMs
a. 600 km range threshold provided that non-nuclear ALCMs
such as Tacit Rainbow are excluded from-START limits.
: (p); |
. b. Except as provided in, subparagraph (d) below, current
and future U.S. heavy bombers equipped for nucleararmed ALCMs will be counted at 10 nuclear-armed ALCMs
per bomber and will not be equipped for more than 20
nuclear-armed ALCMs per bomber. | (Pf)
c.. Except as provided in subparagraph (d) below, current
. and future Soviet heavy bombers equipped for nuclear-
. -armed ALCMs will be counted at 8 nuclear-armed ALCMs.
per bomber and will not be equipped for more than 12:
nuclear-armed ALCMs per bomber. (9)
SHERET:.

we Cre RET
SRCREE 2
. d. Subject to a mutually acceptable definition of "as
: equipped," the U.S. may agree that any U.S. heavy
bombers equipped for nuclear-armed ALCMs in excess of
150-180 will count against the 6000 warhead limit on an
"as equipped" basis. Soviet heavy bombers equipped for
nuclear-armed ALCMs in excess of 140 percent of the
150-180 threshold also will count against the 6000
warhead limit on an "as.equipped" basis. (#)
e. As an alternative to subparagraph (d), the U.S. may
accept a sublimit on the. number of U.S. heavy bombers equipped for nuclear-armed ALCMs that adequately
protects options for the duration of the START Treaty.
Under this approach, the Soviets may deploy up to 40
percent more heavy bombers equipped for nuclear-armed
j ALCMs than the U.S., subject to other START
' constraints, including the 1600/6000 START limits. (Z)
. f. The U.S. requires a continuing right to convert up to
100 non-modern heavy bombers to "former heavy bombers"
. that would not be counted within the 1600/6000 START
aggregates. The U.S. may propose that ""non-modern"
. heavy bombers be defined as being those of a type that
7 has been deployed longer than 10-15 years. )
SLCMs_;
As part of the ALCM/SLCM package, the U.S. may propose that:
. each side make a declaration of policy concerning nuclear-
: armed SLCMs with ranges in excess of 600 km that includes
the following elements:.
a. A statement that the declaration will be politically
: binding. (2)
~ dD. A specification of the maximum number of nuclear-armed
SLCMs that will be deployed for each of the next five
:: mn This specification would be updated annually.
(B)
c. A statement that no more than 875-1000 nuclear~-armed
SLCMs will be deployed at any one time for the duration
of the START Treaty. (jf)
d. Information about which types of surface ships and;
submarines are capable of deploying nuclear-armed
SLCMs. '(P/)

e, An annual exchange of data on nuclear SLCMs with ranges
_ between 300 and 600 km. (9);;
f. A statement that nuclear-armed SLCMs with multiple
. x _independently targeted warheads will not be produced or
' 'deployed. (f) oO
2. Verifying Non-Deployed Mobile Missile Production. The U.S.
may offer an''approach to Perimeter Portal Continuous Monitoring
(PPCM)' based on the following principles:
, a. Numerical reciprocity. (f)
b. Up to approximately four facilities in each country may
be subject to PPCM. - (#).
c. The Soviet facilities at which PPCM should be '
established is a combination of up to approximately
four solid propellant: rocket. motor production.
facilities and final assembly facilities that would:
give us the highest confidence in monitoring the
production of mobile ICBMs. (f)
Solid rocket motor production facilities in both countries at:
which PPCM is not established should be subject to an annual
'quota of mandatory, short-notice inspections if they are
determined to be capable of producing first-stage motors for
mobile ICBMs. Ali other solid rocket motor production facilities
in both countries at which PPCM has not been established should.
be subject to periodic, scheduled: tours to confirm that they have
not been given the capability to produce first-stage motors for
_ mobile ICBMs. (f)
The U.S. and Soviet Union should first exchange lists of all
their facilities that currently produce first stagé motors of
mobile ICBMs and other facilities at.which they are prepared to
accept' PPCM. These lists will be mutually agreed. The two sides
_ will 'then agree to-establish PPCM at specified facilities on
these lists. (f). Lod
The U.S. and Soviet Union will also exchange lists of all solid
propellant rocket motor production facilities. Following the identification of those facilities at which PPCM will be
established, an exchange of data'and one-time tours of all solid
propellant rocket motor production facilities in both countries
should be conducted to determine which facilities will be subject
to. mandatory short-notice inspections, and which will be subject
to periodic, scheduled tours. (f)

, In order to implement this regime in the Soviet Union as soon as
possible, the U.S. may accept the simultaneous implementation of
such a regime in the United States, even if mobile ICBMs are not
yet in production in the U.S. at that time. (3)
. The U.S. should propose that questions about activities at
facilities that are not subject to either short-notice
inspections or periodic, scheduled tours may be raised in the
'Joint Compliance and Inspection Commission (JCIC). The two sides
may agree in the JCIC to inspect such' facilities to aid in
resolving any compliance concerns. hh
3.. Limits on Heavy ICBMs. On the understanding that the U.S.
will seek the ultimate elimination of heavy ICBMs in follow-on
negotiations, the U.S. may accept a package of constraints on
heavy. ICBMs consisting of:
a. A maximum of 1540 RVs on 154. heavy ICBMs. (9)
b. A ban on new types of heavy ICBMs. i)
c. A ban on mobile heavy ICBMs. (J)
d. Such limitations on the modernization of existing types
' of heavy ICBMs as may be agreed between the two sides.
(P) -
4. Limits on Mobile ICBMs and Mobile ICBM RVs. The U.S. should
propose a subceiling of 800-1200 on the number of deployed mobile
. ICBM RVS permitted in START. The U.S. will not seek a subceiling
} on the number of deployed mobile ICBMs permitted in) START. (9)
' ~ Be Backfire.. The Intelligence Community has determined that
the Backfire is an intermediate-range bomber and that the Soviets
. plan to employ it in a theater-role. Although the Backfire,
under certain conditions, has a technical capability to conduct
strikes against targets in the U.S., the Intelligence Community
notes that the Soviets do not plan and would be unlikely to
. employ Backfire in such a role. (F-4)
Accordingly, the U.S. should address the Backfire issue by
: seeking Soviet undertakings in the START context that would
provide added confidence that it will remain dedicated to theater
missions, and will not be employed as a strategic bomber. These
should include, at a minimum, an undertaking that Backfire will
not be given a capability to operate at intercontinental ranges.
| SBeRB2. CrP DRT Bush Library Photocery:

5.:
Additionally, the U.S. should seek a Soviet commitment to include
all Backfires based in the ATTU region in CFE limits on aircraft.
(8:

THE SECRETARY OF STATE

SUBJECT: Decisions on START Issues (U)
At the upcoming U.S.-Soviet summit, I hope to reach substantive
agreement on all of the major START issues on a basis that will
advance U.S. national security interests and promote
international stability. The following decisions are designed to
help achieve that goal by making a best faith effort to resolve
the issues that they address. (Z)
al ALCMs and SLCMs. Building on current areas of agreement
with the Soviet Union and existing guidance, the U.S. can accept
a package that includes the following elements:
ALCMs
a. 600 km range threshold provided that non-nuclear ALCMs
such as Tacit Rainbow are excluded from START limits.
(8)
b. Except as provided in subparagraph (d) below, current
and future U.S. heavy bombers equipped for nucleararmed ALCMs will be counted at 10 nuclear-armed ALCMs
per bomber and will not be equipped for more than 20
nuclear-armed ALCMs per bomber. (J)
Declassify on: OADR

Gs Except as provided in subparagraph (d) below, current
and future Soviet heavy bombers equipped for nucleararmed ALCMs will be counted at 8 nuclear-armed ALCMs
per bomber and will not be equipped for more than 12
nuclear-armed ALCMs per bomber. (p)
d. Subject to a mutually acceptable definition of "as
equipped," the U.S. may agree that any U.S. heavy
bombers equipped for nuclear-armed ALCMs in excess of
150-180 will count against the 6000 warhead limit on an
"as equipped" basis. Soviet heavy bombers equipped for
nuclear-armed ALCMs in excess of 140 percent of the
150-180 threshold also will count against the 6000
warhead limit on an "as equipped" basis. (x)
e. As an alternative to subparagraph (d), the U.S. may
accept a sublimit on the number of U.S. heavy bombers
equipped for nuclear-armed ALCMs that adequately
protects options for the duration of the START Treaty.
Under this approach, the Soviets may deploy up to 40
percent more heavy bombers equipped for nuclear-armed
ALCMs than the U.S., subject to other START
constraints, including the 1600/6000 START limits. (9%)
f. The U.S. requires a continuing right to convert up to
100 non-modern heavy bombers to "former heavy bombers"
that would not be counted within the 1600/6000 START
aggregates. The U.S. may propose that "non-modern"
heavy bombers be defined as being those of a type that
has been deployed longer than 10-15 years. (v
SLCMs
As part of the ALCM/SLCM package, the U.S. may propose that
each side make a declaration of policy concerning nucleararmed SLCMs with ranges in excess of 600 km that includes
the following elements:
a. A statement that the declaration will be politically
binding. (2)
b. A specification of the maximum number of nuclear-armed
SLCMs that will be deployed for each of the next five
years. This specification would be updated annually.
(B)

Gs A statement that no more than 875-1000 nuclear-armed
SLCMs will be deployed at any one time for the duration
of the START Treaty. ()
d. Information about which types of surface ships and
submarines are capable of deploying nuclear-armed
SLCMs. (2)
e. An annual exchange of data on nuclear SLCMs with ranges
between 300 and 600 km. (2)
f. A statement that nuclear-armed SLCMs with multiple
independently targeted warheads will not be produced or
deployed. ()
2. Verifying Non-Deployed Mobile Missile Production. The U.S.
may offer an approach to Perimeter Portal Continuous Monitoring
(PPCM) based on the following principles:
a. Numerical reciprocity. (f)
Ds Up to approximately four facilities in each country may
be subject to PPCM. (Z)
Ce The Soviet facilities at which PPCM should be
established is a combination of up to approximately
four solid propellant rocket motor production
facilities and final assembly facilities that would
give us the highest confidence in monitoring the
production of mobile ICBMs. (9)
Solid rocket motor production facilities in both countries at
which PPCM is not established should be subject to an annual
quota of mandatory, short-notice inspections if they are
determined to be capable of producing first-stage motors for
mobile ICBMs. All other solid rocket motor production facilities
in both countries at which PPCM has not been established should
be subject to periodic, scheduled tours to confirm that they have
not been given the capability to produce first-stage motors for
mobile ICBMs. (Z)
The U.S. and Soviet Union should first exchange lists of all
their facilities that currently produce first stage motors of
mobile ICBMs and other facilities at which they are prepared to
accept PPCM. These lists will be mutually agreed. The two sides
will then agree to establish PPCM at specified facilities on
these lists. (%)
eee

SBeReE
4
The U.S. and Soviet Union will also exchange lists of all solid
propellant rocket motor production facilities. Following the
identification of those facilities at which PPCM will be
established, an exchange of data and one-time tours of all solid
propellant rocket motor production facilities in both countries
should be conducted to determine which facilities will be subject
to mandatory short-notice inspections, and which will be subject
to periodic, scheduled tours. (ff)
In order to implement this regime in the Soviet Union as soon as
possible, the U.S. may accept the simultaneous implementation of
such a regime in the United States, even if mobile ICBMs are not
yet in production in the U.S. at that time. (J)
The U.S. should propose that questions about activities at
facilities that are not subject to either short-notice
inspections or periodic, scheduled tours may be raised in the
Joint Compliance and Inspection Commission (JCIC). The two sides
may agree in the JCIC to inspect such facilities to aid in
resolving any compliance concerns. (£)
33 Limits on Heavy ICBMs. On the understanding that the U.S.
will seek the ultimate elimination of heavy ICBMs in follow-on
negotiations, the U.S. may accept a package of constraints on
heavy ICBMs consisting of:
a. A maximum of 1540 RVs on 154 heavy ICBMs. (9)
b. A ban on new types of heavy ICBMs. (f)
Cc. A ban on mobile heavy ICBMs. (f)
as Such limitations on the modernization of existing types
of heavy ICBMs as may be agreed between the two sides.
(p)
4. Limits on Mobile ICBMs and Mobile ICBM RVs. The U.S. should
propose a subceiling of 800-1200 on the number of deployed mobile
ICBM RVs permitted in START. The U.S. will not seek a subceiling
on the number of deployed mobile ICBMs permitted in START. (Z)
5. Backfire. The Intelligence Community has determined that
the Backfire is an intermediate-range bomber and that the Soviets
plan to employ it in a theater role. Although the Backfire,
under certain conditions, has a technical capability to conduct

"SEGREP
5
| strikes against targets in the U.S., the Intelligence Community
notes that the Soviets do not plan and would be unlikely to
| employ Backfire in such a role. ($)
Accordingly, the U.S. should address the Backfire issue by
seeking Soviet undertakings in the START context that would
provide added confidence that it will remain dedicated to theater
missions, and will not be employed as a strategic bomber. These
should include, at a minimum, an undertaking that Backfire will
not be given a capability to operate at intercontinental ranges.
Additionally, the U.S. should seek a Soviet commitment to include
all Backfires based in the ATTU region in CFE limits on aircraft.
